section.title: A Lament over Tyre
section.sectionRationale: Having pronounced Tyre's sentence, Ezekiel is now told to take up a lament for her, singing as though at a funeral for a great ship. The chapter catalogs Tyre's builders, crews, soldiers, and trading partners from every corner of the known world in loving, almost admiring detail, only to sink that whole magnificent vessel in a single verse, showing how suddenly all that glory comes to nothing.
section.thesis: Tyre's beauty, wealth, and worldwide trade, described here in exquisite and lingering detail, cannot save her when the LORD's judgment comes; the very splendor that made her proud becomes the cargo that sinks with her into the heart of the sea.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Tyre pictured as a magnificent ship, perfect in beauty and built from the finest materials of many lands; her vast network of trading partners spanning the known world; and the sudden wreck of that whole glorious vessel, mourned by sailors and kings alike.

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Now you, son of man, take up a lament for Tyre.
Tell Tyre, who dwells at the gateway to the sea, merchant of the peoples on many coasts, that this is what the Lord GOD says: You have said, O Tyre, 'I am {{kalil|perfect in beauty}}.'
Your borders are in the heart of the seas; your builders perfected your beauty.
They constructed all your planking with cypress from Senir. They took a cedar from Lebanon to make a mast for you.
Of oaks from Bashan they made your oars; of wood from the coasts of Cyprus they made your deck, inlaid with ivory.
Of embroidered fine linen from Egypt they made your sail, which served as your banner. Of blue and purple from the coasts of Elishah they made your awning.
The men of Sidon and Arvad were your oarsmen. Your men of skill, O Tyre, were there as your captains.
The elders of Gebal were aboard as shipwrights, repairing your leaks. All the ships of the sea and their sailors came alongside to barter for your merchandise.
Men of Persia, Lydia, and Put served as warriors in your army. They hung their shields and helmets on your walls; they gave you splendor.
Men of Arvad and Helech manned your walls all around, and the men of Gammad were in your towers. They hung their shields around your walls; they perfected your beauty.

units.label: Merchant of the peoples
units.note: trading partners from every corner of the earth

verses.text: Tarshish was your merchant because of your great wealth of goods; they exchanged silver, iron, tin, and lead for your wares.
Javan, Tubal, and Meshech were your merchants. They exchanged slaves and bronze utensils for your merchandise.
The men of Beth-togarmah exchanged horses, war horses, and mules for your wares.
The men of Dedan were your clients; many coastlands were your market; they paid you with ivory tusks and ebony.
Aram was your customer because of your many products; they exchanged turquoise, purple, embroidered work, fine linen, coral, and rubies for your wares.
Judah and the land of Israel traded with you; they exchanged wheat from Minnith, cakes and honey, oil and balm for your merchandise.
Because of your many products and your great wealth of goods, Damascus traded with you wine from Helbon, wool from Zahar,
and casks of wine from Izal for your wares. Wrought iron, cassia, and sweet cane were exchanged for your merchandise.
Dedan was your merchant in saddlecloths for riding.
Arabia and all the princes of Kedar were your customers, trading in lambs, rams, and goats.
The merchants of Sheba and Raamah traded with you; for your wares they exchanged gold, the finest of all spices, and precious stones.
Haran, Canneh, and Eden traded with you, and so did the merchants of Sheba, Asshur, and Chilmad.
In your marketplace they traded with you fine garments of blue, embroidered work, and multicolored rugs with cords tightly twisted and knotted.
The ships of Tarshish carried your merchandise. And you were filled with heavy cargo in the heart of the sea.

units.label: Wrecked in the heart of the sea

verses.text: Your oarsmen have brought you onto the high seas, but the {{ruach-qadim|east wind}} will shatter you in the heart of the sea.

Your wealth, wares, and merchandise, your sailors, captains, and shipwrights, your merchants and all the warriors within you, with all the other people on board, will sink into the heart of the sea on the day of your downfall.
The countryside will shake when your sailors cry out.
All who handle the oars will abandon their ships. The sailors and all the captains of the sea will stand on the shore.
They will raise their voices for you and cry out bitterly. They will throw dust on their heads and roll in ashes.
They will shave their heads for you and wrap themselves in sackcloth. They will weep over you with anguish of soul and bitter mourning.
As they wail and mourn over you, they will take up a {{qinah|lament}} for you: 'Who was ever like Tyre, silenced in the middle of the sea?
When your wares went out to sea, you satisfied many nations. You enriched the kings of the earth with your abundant wealth and merchandise.
Now you are shattered by the seas in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and the people among you have gone down with you.
All the people of the coastlands are appalled over you. Their kings shudder with fear; their faces are contorted.
Those who trade among the nations hiss at you; you have come to a horrible end and will be no more.'”

kalil.gloss: Complete, perfect, or whole. Tyre's own boast, 'I am perfect in beauty,' claims a completeness that belongs to God alone; the lament exposes how a beauty built purely from trade, timber, and craftsmanship cannot ultimately hold together against judgment.

ruach-qadim.gloss: The east wind, a hot, destructive wind that in the Old Testament often pictures God's own judgment blowing against human pride, as it dries up Egypt's plague relief in Exodus or scatters Israel's leaders in Jeremiah; here it wrecks Tyre's ship at the very height of her voyage.

qinah.gloss: A funeral dirge or lament, the same poetic form used to mourn the dead. This entire chapter is framed as a qinah for Tyre, sung as though for a person, or a great ship, that has died rather than merely a city that has fallen.

basic.context: This chapter follows directly on Tyre's sentence in chapter 26 with an extended, almost loving description of her glory, pictured as a magnificent trading ship built from the finest materials of Lebanon, Bashan, Cyprus, and Egypt, crewed by skilled sailors from Sidon and Arvad, and supplied by merchants from every corner of the known world, before that whole vessel is suddenly wrecked and sinks in a few devastating verses.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a literal historical description of Tyre's real Mediterranean trading network, cast in the poetic form of a lament for the dead, meant to be sung or recited as a dirge rather than delivered as plain prose. The detailed cargo lists describe genuine ancient trade routes and partners; the point of the passage is not merely economic history but the theological lesson that even the most impressive, far reaching human achievement cannot outlast God's judgment.
basic.originalLanguages: Tyre boasts that she is kalil, perfect or complete, a completeness that properly belongs to God alone. Her downfall comes at the hand of the ruach qadim, the east wind, a recurring biblical picture of God's own judgment blowing against human pride. And the whole chapter is framed as a qinah, a funeral lament, treating Tyre's fall with the weight and grief normally reserved for the death of a person.
basic.typology: The ship 'perfect in beauty,' built from the finest materials of many nations yet unable to survive the wind of judgment, anticipates the wider biblical pattern of merchant Babylon in Revelation 18, whose fall is mourned in strikingly similar language by kings, merchants, and sailors, showing that no worldly system of trade and wealth, however global, escapes final judgment before Christ's return.
basic.christ: Tyre's self sufficient boast, 'I am perfect in beauty,' apart from God, is answered by the God who alone truly gives and sustains beauty, wisdom, and stability; where Tyre's borrowed splendor sinks in a moment, Christ offers a kingdom that, unlike Tyre's, 'cannot be shaken,' Hebrews 12:28, built not on trade routes but on His own finished work.
basic.god: God is sovereign not only over armies but over trade, wealth, and the whole interconnected economy of the ancient world; He can name every trading partner, every cargo, every craftsman's homeland, and still bring the entire enterprise down with a single wind, showing His intimate knowledge of and authority over even the most complex human systems.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 23 gives an earlier oracle against Tyre focused on her trade and profit. Revelation 18:9-19 echoes this chapter's precise imagery, merchants, kings, and sailors weeping over a fallen trading empire, applied there to Babylon. Jeremiah 18:17 and Job 27:21 use the same east wind image for sudden, devastating judgment. James 4:13-16 warns against boasting in tomorrow as though wealth and plans were guaranteed, a New Testament echo of Tyre's presumption.
basic.oneStory: Tyre's glittering, worldwide trade could not save her from the wind of judgment, and her fall is mourned as though a great ship, even a person, had died. This anticipates the whole Bible's pattern that every impressive human achievement, apart from God, will finally sink, while the kingdom built on Christ alone remains when every other splendor has gone down into the sea.

context.literary: A single sustained lament, or qinah, following directly on chapter 26's sentence against Tyre, moving from an extended, admiring description of Tyre as a magnificent ship built and crewed from the finest resources of many lands, through a long catalog of her trading partners, to a sudden, brief account of her wreck and the grief of sailors and kings who watch her sink.
context.historical: The chapter's cargo lists reflect genuine Phoenician trade networks reaching from Tarshish, likely in Spain, to Sheba in the south of Arabia and Javan, Tubal, and Meshech in Asia Minor, matching what is known from other ancient Near Eastern sources about Tyre's real position as the Mediterranean's premier trading hub in the centuries before her fall to Babylon and later Alexander.
context.canonical: The second of three chapters devoted to Tyre, 26 to 28, and the most extensive lament for any single city or nation in the Old Testament nation oracles, later echoed closely by Revelation 18's lament over Babylon's commercial fall.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To mourn, in the traditional form of a funeral dirge, the sudden and total collapse of Tyre's glittering worldwide trade empire, showing both the genuine, almost admirable scale of her achievement and the certainty of her fall before God's judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes, in poetic lament form, the real historical scope and eventual collapse of Tyre's trading network; it does not prescribe a specific economic policy or condemn trade itself. Its abiding principle is that wealth and achievement, however vast or admired, offer no ultimate security apart from God.

debates.question: Why does Ezekiel describe Tyre's glory in such loving, extensive, almost admiring detail, twenty five verses of careful cargo lists and craftsmanship, before her fall, rather than moving quickly to judgment?

views.label: The extended praise heightens the tragedy and the lesson of the lament form
views.case: A qinah, a funeral lament, traditionally recalls the full glory and virtues of the deceased before mourning the loss; the lingering, admiring detail is not approval of Tyre's pride but a deliberate literary technique making her sudden, total collapse feel as devastating to the reader as it would to those who depended on her.

views.label: The detail shows God's genuine appreciation for human skill and craftsmanship, judged only where it turns to pride
views.case: The passage's warmth toward Tyre's genuinely impressive shipbuilding, trade networks, and artisanship reflects that such skill is itself a good gift, reflecting the image of a creative God; the judgment falls not on craftsmanship or trade as such but on the self sufficient boast, 'I am perfect in beauty,' that forgot the giver of every skill being described.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that the passage does not condemn skill, trade, or beauty themselves; the tragedy is that such genuine goods were finally trusted in for their own sake rather than received with gratitude toward the God who gives every skilled hand its ability.

study.originalLanguages: Tyre boasts she is kalil, perfect or complete, a wholeness that belongs to God alone yet which she claims for herself through trade and craftsmanship. Her sudden ruin comes by the ruach qadim, the east wind, a recurring biblical figure for God's own judgment against pride. And the entire chapter is shaped as a qinah, a funeral lament, mourning a city, and a ship, as though for the death of a person.

typology.type: The magnificent ship 'perfect in beauty,' built from many nations yet wrecked by the wind of judgment
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates merchant Babylon's fall in Revelation 18, mourned in strikingly similar language, showing that no worldly system of trade escapes final judgment before Christ returns to establish His unshakable kingdom, Hebrews 12:28.

study.christ: Tyre's self sufficient boast, 'I am perfect in beauty,' claims for herself a completeness that belongs to God alone; her borrowed splendor, built from the timber and skill of many nations, sinks in a moment when judgment comes. Christ offers what Tyre could not build for herself: a kingdom that, unlike her trading empire, 'cannot be shaken,' Hebrews 12:28, founded not on cargo and craftsmanship but on His own finished, unshakeable work.
study.god: God's sovereignty reaches into the smallest details of international trade and craftsmanship, He can name every partner nation and every cargo, and still bring the whole magnificent enterprise down with a single wind. His judgment is neither hasty nor uninformed; He knows precisely what He is bringing low, and precisely why.

crossReferences.note: An earlier oracle against Tyre, focused on her trade and the eventual promise that her restored profit would be set apart to the LORD.
Merchants, kings, and sailors weep over fallen Babylon in language strikingly similar to this chapter's lament for Tyre.
God scatters His people 'like the east wind' before their enemies, the same image of sudden, devastating judgment used here against Tyre.
Warns against boasting confidently in tomorrow's plans and wealth, echoing the presumption of Tyre's claim to be 'perfect in beauty.'

study.oneStory: Tyre's glittering, worldwide trade network could not save her from the wind of God's judgment, and her sudden fall is mourned as though a great ship, even a person, had died. This anticipates the whole Bible's pattern that every impressive human achievement, however vast or admired, will finally sink apart from God, while the kingdom built on Christ alone remains standing when every other splendor has gone down into the sea.

quiz.q: How is Tyre pictured throughout most of this chapter?

options.text: As a magnificent trading ship, built from the finest materials of many nations and crewed by skilled sailors and craftsmen.
options.note: Verses 4 to 11 describe her planking, mast, oars, sail, and crew all drawn from named regions, forming this extended ship metaphor.

options.text: As a fortified mountain city under siege.
options.note: This chapter's central image is a ship, not a mountain fortress; the siege imagery belongs to chapter 26.

options.text: As a single powerful king ruling alone.
options.note: The focus here is on Tyre's collective trade and craftsmanship, not a single ruler; the king of Tyre is addressed separately in chapter 28.

options.text: As a barren wilderness overtaken by wild animals.
options.note: The dominant image is a glorious, richly furnished ship, not a desolate wilderness.

quiz.q: What does the long list of trading partners in verses 12 to 25 emphasize?

options.text: The vast, worldwide scale of Tyre's commercial network, reaching from Tarshish to Sheba to Javan.
options.note: The list names partners from Spain to Arabia to Asia Minor, underscoring truly global reach for the ancient world.

options.text: That Tyre traded only with her immediate neighbors.
options.note: The opposite is true; the list spans an extraordinarily wide geographic range of partners.

options.text: That Tyre's trade was entirely illegal under international law.
options.note: No legal judgment on the trade itself is made in this list; it simply catalogs partners and goods.

options.text: That Tyre's economy depended on a single trading partner.
options.note: The list names many distinct partners and goods, showing broad diversification, not dependence on one source.

quiz.q: What causes Tyre's sudden downfall in verse 26?

options.text: The east wind shatters her in the heart of the sea.
options.note: Verse 26 names the east wind directly as the force that wrecks her at the height of her voyage.

options.text: A slow decline in trade over many decades.
options.note: The text describes a sudden wreck, not a gradual economic decline.

options.text: An internal mutiny among her own sailors.
options.note: No mutiny is described; the destructive force named is the east wind, a recognized image for divine judgment.

options.text: A fire that started within the ship itself.
options.note: The specific cause named in the text is the east wind, not an internal fire.

quiz.q: How do the sailors and kings respond to Tyre's wreck, according to verses 29 to 35?

options.text: They abandon ship, cry out bitterly, throw dust on their heads, and are appalled and shaken with fear.
options.note: Verses 29 to 35 describe exactly this range of mourning and dread among sailors and kings alike.

options.text: They quickly rebuild a new ship to replace her.
options.note: No rebuilding effort is described; the response is entirely one of grief and horror.

options.text: They celebrate the removal of a trade competitor.
options.note: The text describes deep mourning and terror, the opposite of celebration.

options.text: They remain calm, viewing it as a routine business loss.
options.note: The described reactions, bitter weeping, dust, shaved heads, are far stronger than a routine, calm response.

quiz.q: What is the chapter's underlying lesson about wealth, beauty, and human achievement?

options.text: Even the most impressive, far reaching human achievement cannot outlast God's judgment when it becomes a source of self sufficient pride.
options.note: Tyre's own boast, 'I am perfect in beauty,' verse 3, is answered by total collapse, tying her pride directly to her fall.

options.text: Wealth and trade are inherently sinful and should be avoided entirely.
options.note: The passage does not condemn trade or craftsmanship itself, but rather the self sufficient pride built upon them.

options.text: Only nations without any natural resources are ever judged by God.
options.note: The opposite is shown; Tyre is judged precisely because of her greatness and pride in her resources and trade.

options.text: Human skill and craftsmanship have no value in God's eyes.
options.note: The chapter describes Tyre's craftsmanship with real appreciation for its detail and skill; the judgment falls on pride, not on skill itself.

questions: Tyre's boast was 'I am perfect in beauty,' built entirely from her own trade and craftsmanship. What accomplishment or possession are you tempted to credit entirely to your own skill rather than to God's provision?

The chapter lingers lovingly over Tyre's genuine skill and global reach before describing her sudden collapse. Where has something genuinely good in your life, a skill, a relationship, a role, quietly become the thing you trust in instead of God?

The east wind wrecked Tyre 'in the heart of the sea,' at the very height of her success. Have you experienced a sudden reversal at a moment when things seemed most secure, and how did that shape your trust in God afterward?

The sailors and kings who once profited from Tyre now mourn her as though she were a person who had died. Who or what in your life have you depended on in ways that, if lost, would leave a grief this deep, and how does that compare to your dependence on God?

Unlike Tyre's trading empire, Christ offers a kingdom that 'cannot be shaken.' What would it look like this week to consciously build your security on Christ rather than on achievements, income, or reputation that could be swept away by a single wind?

translationNotes.note: Tyre's own claim, 'I am perfect in beauty,' uses the Hebrew kalil, complete or whole, a term that elsewhere in Scripture is reserved for describing wholehearted devotion or completeness properly belonging to God, making her boast especially striking.
The BSB text notes uncertainty in the precise identification of some place names in this verse, such as Izal; the general sense of extensive trade in fine goods is not affected.

The closing phrase, 'you have come to a horrible end and will be no more,' matches almost word for word the ending of the lament over the king of Tyre in 28:19, tying this chapter and the next together as a single extended meditation on Tyre's fall.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Ezekiel 27:1-36. No verified sermon from the trusted cluster directly expounding this specific passage was found this session; sermon status is none.
